name: flask-migrate

metadata.name: Flask-Migrate
metadata.summary: SQLAlchemy database migrations for Flask applications using Alembic.

documentation:
Flask-Migrate
=============

[![Build status](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/flask-migrate/workflows/build/badge.svg)](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/flask-migrate/actions)

Flask-Migrate is an extension that handles SQLAlchemy database migrations for Flask applications using Alembic. The database operations are provided as command-line arguments under the `flask db` command.

Installation
------------

Install Flask-Migrate with `pip`:

    pip install Flask-Migrate

Example
-------

This is an example application that handles database migrations through Flask-Migrate:

```python
from flask import Flask
from flask_sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy
from flask_migrate import Migrate

app = Flask(__name__)
app.config['SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI'] = 'sqlite:///app.db'

db = SQLAlchemy(app)
migrate = Migrate(app, db)

class User(db.Model):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = db.Column(db.String(128))
```

With the above application you can create the database or enable migrations if the database already exists with the following command:

    $ flask db init

Note that the `FLASK_APP` environment variable must be set according to the Flask documentation for this command to work. This will add a `migrations` folder to your application. The contents of this folder need to be added to version control along with your other source files. 

You can then generate an initial migration:

    $ flask db migrate
    
The migration script needs to be reviewed and edited, as Alembic currently does not detect every change you make to your models. In particular, Alembic is currently unable to detect indexes. Once finalized, the migration script also needs to be added to version control.

Then you can apply the migration to the database:

    $ flask db upgrade
    
Then each time the database models change repeat the `migrate` and `upgrade` commands.

To sync the database in another system just refresh the `migrations` folder from source control and run the `upgrade` command.

To see all the commands that are available run this command:

    $ flask db --help

Resources
---------

- [Documentation](http://flask-migrate.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)
- [pypi](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/Flask-Migrate) 
- [Change Log](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/Flask-Migrate/blob/master/CHANGES.md)

documentation: List available templates.

functions.name: flask_migrate.init
documentation: Creates a new migration repository

functions.name: flask_migrate.revision
documentation: Create a new revision file.

functions.name: flask_migrate.migrate
documentation: Alias for 'revision --autogenerate'

functions.name: flask_migrate.edit
documentation: Edit current revision.

functions.name: flask_migrate.merge
documentation: Merge two revisions together.  Creates a new migration file

functions.name: flask_migrate.upgrade
documentation: Upgrade to a later version

functions.name: flask_migrate.downgrade
documentation: Revert to a previous version

functions.name: flask_migrate.show
documentation: Show the revision denoted by the given symbol.

functions.name: flask_migrate.history
documentation: List changeset scripts in chronological order.

functions.name: flask_migrate.heads
documentation: Show current available heads in the script directory

functions.name: flask_migrate.branches
documentation: Show current branch points

functions.name: flask_migrate.current
documentation: Display the current revision for each database.

functions.name: flask_migrate.stamp
documentation:
'stamp' the revision table with the given revision; don't run any
migrations

functions.name: flask_migrate.check
documentation: Check if there are any new operations to migrate

methods.name: flask_migrate._MigrateConfig.metadata
documentation:
Backwards compatibility, in old releases app.extensions['migrate']
was set to db, and env.py accessed app.extensions['migrate'].metadata

functions.name: flask_migrate.templates.aioflask-multidb.env.get_metadata
documentation: Return the metadata for a bind.
other values from the config, defined by the needs of env.py,
can be acquired:
my_important_option = config.get_main_option("my_important_option")
... etc.

functions.name: flask_migrate.templates.aioflask-multidb.env.run_migrations_offline
documentation:
Run migrations in 'offline' mode.

This configures the context with just a URL
and not an Engine, though an Engine is acceptable
here as well.  By skipping the Engine creation
we don't even need a DBAPI to be available.

Calls to context.execute() here emit the given string to the
script output.

functions.name: flask_migrate.templates.aioflask-multidb.env.run_migrations_online
documentation:
Run migrations in 'online' mode.

In this scenario we need to create an Engine
and associate a connection with the context.

functions.name: flask_migrate.templates.flask.env.run_migrations_offline
documentation:
Run migrations in 'offline' mode.

This configures the context with just a URL
and not an Engine, though an Engine is acceptable
here as well.  By skipping the Engine creation
we don't even need a DBAPI to be available.

Calls to context.execute() here emit the given string to the
script output.

functions.name: flask_migrate.templates.flask.env.run_migrations_online
documentation:
Run migrations in 'online' mode.

In this scenario we need to create an Engine
and associate a connection with the context.
